name: Elisha
description: Elisha, a prominent prophet in the Hebrew Bible, was the successor to Elijah. His prophetic ministry, documented primarily in the Books of Kings, is characterized by numerous miracles and acts of divine intervention. Elisha's origins are traced back to Abel-meholah, where Elijah found him plowing with twelve yoke of oxen. Upon Elijah's ascension, Elisha inherited his mantle, signifying the transfer of prophetic power. He is renowned for his compassionate deeds, such as purifying water sources, multiplying oil, and raising the dead. He also played a significant role in political and military affairs, advising kings and intervening in battles. Elisha's miracles demonstrated God's power and compassion, solidifying his reputation as a revered prophet. His stories are not just historical accounts but also serve as moral and spiritual lessons, emphasizing faith, obedience, and divine justice. Elisha's legacy continues to inspire and influence Jewish tradition, showcasing the potential for individuals to channel divine power and effect positive change in the world. His prophetic abilities included foresight, healing, and the ability to curse and bless, all demonstrating his profound connection to the divine.
subtitle: Prophet of miracles and divine power
achievements: Causing an axe head to float

keyMyths.title: The Purification of Jericho's Water
keyMyths.description: The water of Jericho was bad, causing barrenness. Elisha purified the water source by throwing salt into it, making it wholesome and fertile. This act demonstrated his power to heal and restore.

keyMyths.title: The Multiplication of the Widow's Oil
keyMyths.description: A widow was about to lose her sons to debt bondage. Elisha miraculously multiplied her small amount of oil, allowing her to sell it and pay her debts. This showed his compassion and ability to provide for the needy.

keyMyths.title: The Raising of the Shunammite Woman's Son
keyMyths.description: Elisha promised a barren Shunammite woman a son, and when the child died, Elisha raised him back to life. This is one of Elisha's most famous miracles, demonstrating his power over life and death.

sources.author: The Hebrew Bible
sources.description: Primary source for the stories and accounts of Elisha's life and miracles.
sources.text: Primary source for the stories and accounts of Elisha's life and miracles.

sources.author: Louis Ginzberg
sources.description: A comprehensive compilation of Jewish folklore and traditions, including expanded narratives about Elisha.
sources.text: A comprehensive compilation of Jewish folklore and traditions, including expanded narratives about Elisha.

associations.name: Elijah's Mantle
associations.significance: Represents the transfer of prophetic power and authority from Elijah to Elisha.

associations.name: Oxen
associations.significance: Symbolize Elisha's former life as a farmer and his humble beginnings before becoming a prophet.

associations.name: Miracles
associations.significance: Highlight Elisha's divine connection and ability to intervene in the natural world.

associations.name: Abel-meholah
associations.significance: Marks Elisha's origin and the location where Elijah first encountered him.

associations.name: Staff
associations.significance: Used in several of Elisha's miracles, representing divine power and authority.

associations.name: Pot of Stew
associations.significance: Represents Elisha's ability to purify and heal, as demonstrated when he purified the poisoned stew.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Both Asclepius and Elisha are known for their healing abilities and miraculous interventions to cure the sick and afflicted.
Similar to how Elisha was a protector figure for the Israelites, Heimdallr is a guardian figure, watching over and protecting Asgard from invaders.
Both Imhotep and Elisha are revered for their wisdom, healing abilities, and contributions to their respective societies, often being elevated to semi-divine status.

worshipPractices.name: Remembrance
worshipPractices.description: Elisha is remembered and honored in Jewish tradition through the reading and study of his stories in the Books of Kings. His miracles and teachings are often cited as examples of divine power and prophetic insight.

cultural.modernLegacy: Elisha's stories continue to inspire faith and provide moral lessons in both Jewish and Christian traditions. He is regarded as a symbol of divine power, prophetic calling, and the importance of faith and obedience to God.

extendedContent.title: Early Life and Calling
extendedContent.content: Elisha's origins are humble, coming from the town of Abel-meholah, where he was found plowing with twelve yoke of oxen, indicating he came from a family of means. The prophet Elijah, under divine instruction, sought Elisha to be his successor. Elijah cast his mantle upon Elisha, a symbolic act of transferring prophetic authority. Elisha immediately left his former life, slaughtering the oxen, using their wood to cook the meat, and providing a farewell feast for his people, demonstrating a complete commitment to his new calling. He then followed Elijah and became his attendant, learning the ways of a prophet and preparing for the responsibilities that lay ahead. This transition signifies a profound shift from a life of agricultural labor to one of spiritual leadership and divine service.

extendedContent.title: Greatest Quests and Miracles
extendedContent.content: Elisha's ministry is marked by numerous miracles, solidifying his reputation as a powerful prophet. After Elijah's ascension, Elisha performed his first miracle by parting the Jordan River, mirroring Elijah's earlier feat. He purified the water supply of Jericho, ending a long-standing curse. He multiplied a widow's oil to save her sons from slavery and resurrected a Shunammite woman's son, demonstrating his power over life and death. Elisha also healed Naaman, the Syrian commander, of leprosy, which led to Naaman's conversion to the God of Israel. These acts demonstrated not only divine power but also compassion and justice, reinforcing his role as a protector and benefactor of the people of Israel. His miracles also served as a testament to the power of faith and obedience to God's word.

extendedContent.title: Legacy and Influence
extendedContent.content: Elisha's legacy extends beyond his miraculous deeds, influencing later prophetic traditions and religious thought. His stories are preserved in the Hebrew Bible, serving as moral and spiritual lessons for generations. He is considered a pivotal figure in the history of Israel, bridging the gap between the prophetic ministries of Elijah and later prophets. Elisha's emphasis on social justice, compassion, and divine intervention continues to resonate within Jewish and Christian theology. He is remembered as a symbol of faith, obedience, and the power of divine calling. His image persists as a model for religious leaders and a reminder of the potential for ordinary individuals to achieve extraordinary things through faith and commitment to a higher purpose. The tales of his miracles provide hope and inspiration, reinforcing the belief in divine providence and the possibility of overcoming adversity through faith.

shortDescription: Elisha was a prophet and miracle worker in the Hebrew Bible, succeeding Elijah. Known for healing, resurrections, and parting the Jordan River.
